stories.headline: Update: Broncos linebacker Jonathon Cooper arrested second time in a week; forensic exam reveals strangulation injury to girlfriend
stories.summary:
Denver Broncos outside linebacker Jonathon Cooper was arrested Thursday night for the second time in roughly a week, this time on misdemeanor charges of harassment and violation of a protection order after allegedly showing up at his girlfriend's apartment and sending more than 20 messages in defiance of a court order. The new arrest follows his original June 4 detention on second-degree felony and third-degree misdemeanor charges filed in Douglas County District Court.

A forensic nurse who examined Cooper's girlfriend after the initial incident reported that the woman experienced "strangulation with hypoxia and traumatic brain injury," according to an updated arrest affidavit obtained by The Denver Post. The woman told police Cooper grabbed and lifted her by the neck, threw her to the ground multiple times, and pressed her against a wall during the June 4 altercation at his apartment.

Cooper appeared in Douglas County court Friday morning, where Judge Mark Solomon issued a $5,000 personal recognizance bond. The court ordered Cooper to obtain prior approval before traveling outside Colorado; the Broncos are scheduled to play a preseason game in Atlanta on Aug. 14. The Broncos said they were "disappointed" by the arrest and are continuing to review the matter.

Under the NFL's personal conduct policy, players found guilty of criminal assault through a league investigation face a baseline suspension of up to six games. An NFL spokesperson confirmed the matter remains under review of the personal conduct policy. Denver Post columnist Mark Kiszla called on the Broncos and head coach Sean Payton to release Cooper, citing a $5.7 million cap hit as secondary to franchise accountability.

stories.headline: RTD board advances plans for up to 20% cut in bus and train service starting in 2027
stories.summary:
The Regional Transportation District board voted unanimously Wednesday night to direct agency planners to identify specific service reductions of 15%, 17.5%, and 20% in bus and train operations, with cuts potentially taking effect in 2027. The move marks a significant shift toward accepting managers' earlier recommendation as RTD works to address a $215 million annual budget deficit within its $1.5 billion budget.

A 20% reduction would eliminate an estimated 7,300 hours of monthly service across RTD's 2,345-square-mile network and save approximately $62 million per year. Planners are expected to present proposed combinations of specific route, frequency, and hour reductions later this month, with early morning, late-night, and weekend runs considered likely targets.

Board members acknowledged the cuts risk accelerating a ridership decline that has already reached nearly 40% since 2019, but said no other options sustainably close the budget gap. Director Chris Nicholson argued reductions should target underutilized runs to minimize impact on regular riders, while Director Julien Bouquet said the board must "come to the hard reality."

RTD's chief financial officer warned that failure to balance the 2027 budget could prompt financial institutions to downgrade the agency's credit rating, jeopardizing funds needed for track and fleet maintenance. Board members expressed hope that a potential 2028 ballot measure seeking a voter-approved tax increase could eventually restore and expand service if cuts are made now.

stories.headline: E-bike rider dies after crash on eastbound I-70 in Denver's Elyria Swansea neighborhood
stories.summary:
An e-bike cyclist died after a collision with a vehicle near Steele Street and eastbound Interstate 70 in Denver's Elyria Swansea neighborhood Thursday evening, the Denver Police Department confirmed. Officers reported the crash at approximately 7:24 p.m. and the rider was transported to a hospital, where they were later pronounced dead.

Denver police said Friday that the rider was attempting to flee from law enforcement at the time of the crash, according to a statement from DPD. The investigation remains ongoing and no further details about the circumstances of the police interaction were immediately released.

The stretch of I-70 through Elyria Swansea has been a focus of community concern for years due to high traffic volumes and limited pedestrian and cyclist infrastructure in the surrounding neighborhood. The identity of the rider has not yet been publicly released.

stories.headline: Denver metro housing market slows sharply; closed sales fall nearly 7% year-over-year as interest rates squeeze affordability
stories.summary:
The Denver metro housing market showed significant signs of fatigue in May, with closed sales falling 4.32% month-over-month and nearly 7% compared to May 2025, according to the Denver Metro Association of Realtors' monthly report. The median home price reached $615,000 in May, up 2% from April, but new listings dropped 9% from the prior month even as active inventory grew 6% to 12,259 homes — largely because properties are sitting on the market longer.

DMAR market trends committee chair Amanda Snitker identified interest rates as the primary driver of the affordability crisis. A buyer who purchased at Denver's median price in March 2020 at a 3.8% rate paid roughly $1,866 per month; that same home at today's median price and a 6.5% rate now carries a monthly payment of approximately $3,498 — an 87% increase in six years.

The entry-level segment has been hit hardest, with closed sales of condos and townhomes dropping nearly 18% year-over-year. Homeowners locked into low-rate mortgages face monthly payment increases of $1,500 to $2,000 if they move, keeping many would-be sellers on the sidelines even if they want to list. The $500,000 to $749,999 detached home range remains the market's most active tier, with just 2.25 months of supply.

The luxury segment above $1 million is bucking broader trends, with closed sales up 5% month-over-month and 6% year-over-year as wealthier buyers remain insulated from rate sensitivity. Industry experts expect mortgage rates to gradually decline later in 2026 but forecast they will remain well above recent historic lows, keeping pressure on affordability across most price points.

stories.headline: Two Jefferson County Democrats compete to replace term-limited Secretary of State Jena Griswold
stories.summary:
With Secretary of State Jena Griswold term-limited and unable to seek re-election, two Jefferson County Democrats are competing in the June 30 primary to succeed her in one of Colorado's most closely watched statewide races. The contest will determine who leads the office responsible for overseeing elections, business registrations, and campaign finance enforcement.

Griswold has been one of Colorado's most prominent statewide officials, gaining national attention for her election administration during the 2020 and 2022 cycles. The primary outcome will set up what could be a competitive general election contest in November, as Republicans look to make gains in statewide offices for the first time since 2016.

The June 30 primary also features contested races for other statewide offices, including the attorney general's seat, creating a significant election day for Colorado Democrats seeking to maintain their hold on the executive branch ahead of the November general election.

stories.headline: Man sexually assaults teen hours after release from custody, later arrested in Denver metro and extradited to Fort Morgan
stories.summary:
Fort Morgan police investigators say a man sexually assaulted a teenager within hours of his release from a metro-area jail, according to Fox31 Denver. Investigators determined the man was not living at his registered address following release, obtained an arrest warrant, and later located and arrested him in the Denver metro area.

The suspect was extradited to Morgan County to face charges. Authorities did not immediately release the suspect's name or the specific circumstances of his release, but the case has drawn attention to supervision protocols following jail release in Colorado.

The incident is under investigation by Fort Morgan police. No further details about the victim or the timeline of events were released publicly.

stories.headline: University of Denver announces major academic restructuring, closing five departments amid budget pressures
stories.summary:
The University of Denver announced a significant academic restructuring that includes the closure of five departments as the institution grapples with budget pressures. The move represents one of the more sweeping academic reorganizations at a major Colorado university in recent years and is expected to affect faculty, staff, and enrolled students.

University officials have not publicly detailed which specific departments are being eliminated or the projected financial savings from the consolidation. Such restructurings at private universities typically involve merging programs into larger colleges or schools to reduce administrative overhead and consolidate resources.

The announcement is likely to prompt concern among faculty governance bodies and students in affected programs about course availability, degree completion pathways, and faculty employment. DU joins a growing list of American universities undertaking structural cuts as enrollment pressures and rising operational costs strain higher education finances nationwide.

stories.headline: Red Bull Soap Box Race to return to Denver's Empower Field after 18-year absence; Mayor Johnston to compete
stories.summary:
The Red Bull Soap Box Race will return to Denver's Empower Field at Mile High after an 18-year hiatus, following a successful city bid to host the amateur gravity-racing event. Denver Mayor Mike Johnston is among the participants who will compete in the race, which features homemade non-motorized vehicles navigating a purpose-built downhill course.

The Soap Box Race is a global Red Bull event that combines engineering creativity with spectacle, typically drawing large crowds and media attention to host cities. The return of the race to Denver marks a notable civic and entertainment event for the city, which previously hosted the competition in 2008.

Details on the specific race date and registration for additional competitors were not immediately available, but the event is expected to draw significant public interest given the venue's prominence and the mayor's participation.

stories.headline: Denver Health named to Newsweek's best maternity hospitals list; city to close offices for Juneteenth; City Park Bandstand rebuild advances
stories.summary:
Denver Health has been named to Newsweek's America's Best Maternity Hospitals 2026 list, a national recognition honoring exceptional care from pregnancy through the postpartum period. The hospital delivers approximately one in three babies born in Denver and serves as the primary safety-net health system for nearly a quarter of the city's population, according to officials.

Most Denver city agencies and offices will close June 19 in observance of the Juneteenth holiday. Denver International Airport, the Denver Sheriff, Police and Fire departments, and other emergency services will remain fully operational.

Denver Parks and Recreation announced a community pop-up event at City Park Pavilion on June 28, where residents can meet the design team and receive updates on plans to rebuild the City Park Bandstand, which was heavily damaged in a fire this past March. Demolition of the remaining unsalvageable structure is planned for fall 2026, with construction of a new bandstand expected to begin by year's end and completion targeted for the 2027 City Park Jazz season.

stories.headline: Denver Summit FC defender Carson Pickett inspires children with limb differences through Hand Camp Colorado partnership
stories.summary:
Denver Summit FC defender Carson Pickett, who was born without her left forearm and hand, has partnered with Hand Camp Colorado — a local nonprofit supporting children with limb differences — as part of her nomination for the NWSL's Lauren Holiday Impact Award. Pickett hosted her first event with the organization at Infinity Park on June 4, where children ages 5 to 8 participated in soccer drills and spent time with the professional defender.

Pickett, the first player with a limb difference to represent the U.S. Women's National Team, said combining her love of soccer with advocacy for children who share her experience has been deeply meaningful. Families traveled from as far as Fort Collins to attend the event, with parents describing the encounter as potentially life-changing for their children.

Hand Camp Colorado President Cailin Delaney said Pickett's lived experience gives her a unique connection to the children the organization serves, emphasizing the importance of community and representation for kids who may feel isolated by their differences. Pickett told one child who asked about being bullied that being unique is something to embrace.

Pickett is already planning a follow-up event, including an Olympics-style day introducing the children to multiple sports. The NWSL Lauren Holiday Impact Award winner will be announced in November during championship week.

stories.headline: Cherry Creek school district under federal investigation for alleged racially discriminatory programming
stories.summary:
The Cherry Creek School District, one of the largest school districts in Colorado, is under federal investigation for allegedly operating racially discriminatory programming, according to reports. The investigation adds to scrutiny facing Colorado educational institutions over program equity and civil rights compliance.

The district serves students across a large swath of the southeastern Denver metro area, including communities in Arapahoe County. Details about the specific programs under review and the federal agency leading the investigation were not fully disclosed in available reporting.

Federal civil rights investigations of school districts can result in required policy changes, loss of federal funding, or negotiated resolution agreements if violations are found. The district has not yet publicly commented in detail on the scope or status of the investigation.

stories.headline: I-70 closed in both directions near Glenwood Springs as Paradise Fire breaks out along corridor
stories.summary:
Interstate 70 was closed in both directions near Glenwood Springs after the Paradise Fire broke out along the highway corridor, disrupting one of Colorado's most critical travel and freight routes through the Rocky Mountains. The closure forced traffic diversions and affected travelers heading between Denver and western Colorado and Utah.

Glenwood Canyon and the surrounding area have a history of fire and flood-related I-70 closures, and the stretch is considered one of the most vulnerable sections of the interstate due to steep terrain and limited alternative routes. The closure comes as Colorado continues to face elevated fire danger statewide.

The duration of the closure and the size of the fire were not immediately specified in available reports. Transportation officials and firefighting crews were responding to the scene, and travelers were advised to check CDOT for updates before attempting to use the corridor.

joke.joke: Buffets are the only place in the world where you can look at food, not want it, and still put it on your plate anyway.

topStory.headline: Workers Remove Trump's Name From Kennedy Center Facade After Courts Reject Emergency Appeals
topStory.summary:
Construction crews began removing President Donald Trump's name from the exterior of the John F. Kennedy Center for the Performing Arts in Washington early Saturday morning, completing the work in the overnight hours after a series of legal efforts to halt the removal failed. Workers erected scaffolding Friday around the section of the building bearing Trump's name, but a thunderstorm delayed operations, prompting the Kennedy Center to ask a judge to extend the court-ordered Friday deadline to noon Saturday. Workers ultimately began the removal shortly after midnight, covering the scaffolding with tarps before taking down the letters, and departed the site around 3:30 a.m.

U.S. District Judge Christopher Cooper had rejected a last-minute request Friday afternoon from the Kennedy Center's board to pause compliance with his removal order. The center then filed an emergency appeal with a federal appeals court in Washington seeking a stay, but that court rejected the appeal Friday evening, shortly before the original deadline. Justice Department lawyers representing Trump and the Kennedy Center's board had filed the appeal, arguing for more time.

Cooper issued his underlying ruling last month, finding that Trump's name had been illegally added to the building. In a 94-page opinion, he wrote that Congress established the Kennedy Center's name and that only Congress has the authority to change it. His order gave the institution two weeks to remove all references to a 'Trump Kennedy Center' from its building, branding, website, and other materials, and also temporarily blocked a planned two-year closure of the facility for a $257 million renovation.

The legal challenge was brought by Rep. Joyce Beatty, a Democrat from Ohio who serves as an ex-officio member of the Kennedy Center's board. Beatty was present on the plaza outside the building Friday evening, where dozens of onlookers gathered to watch the scaffolding go up, chanting 'take it down.' Earlier in the week, the Kennedy Center had already removed Trump's name from its website and email communications in partial compliance with the order.

The dispute stems from actions Trump took early in his second term, when he ousted the center's previous leadership and installed a new board of trustees that named him chairman. Trump's name was added to the building shortly thereafter. The removal of his name marks a significant legal setback for the administration's efforts to reshape the federally chartered performing arts institution, and the broader question of executive authority over the Kennedy Center's governance and operations is likely to continue in the courts.
